data.op_id: Graph embedding and link prediction > Inductive setting > Define inductive PyKEEN model
data.title: Define inductive PyKEEN model

params.text:
### Dataset importing
Here we are importing one of the 5 fully-inductive datasets found in PyKEEN. They all contain the following (as custom datasets also should):
- Transductive training edges -- which is used for training the model, all relations have to appear in this set.
- Inductive inference edges -- this is used for inference, can be a super set of the transductive graph (semi inductive setting) or an entire disjoint graph with unseen entities (fully inductive setting).
- Inductive testing edges -- edges used for evaluating model performance at training time, is disjoint with the inference set.
- Inductive validation edges -- edges used for validating model performance, also disjoint with both inference and testing set.

You could also import your own data like normal with the `Import file` box. And then use the `Split inductive dataset` box, where if you specify `0` as the entity split it will be a semi inductive setting, and if larger than `0` it will be a fully inductive setting.

And then it can be used the same as the output of the `Import inductive dataset` box.

### Model definition
This box defines the model we want to train.
Under the hood this uses the `NodePiece` representation that efficiently stores entity representations leading to much fewer parameters with minimal performance loss.

As of now only models with 1 kind of representations work. So for example `KG2E` will not work as it uses 2 representations (one for the `mean` and the other for `diagonal convergence`) with entities viewed as probability distributions. But something like `TransE` or `DistMult` works as they are translational models and only use distances.

### Prediction
The already defined boxes all work in an inductive setting too, you just have to toggle the `inductive setting` checkmark. Both semi- or fully inductive settings are working.
Prediction flow is the same as in all other link prediction tasks.

### Model evaluation
Evaluation works almost the same, but here we have two different graphs, so we have to provide the inductive inference table. 

Testing and validation tables also have to be provided.

Evaluation will be performed on the validation set, with the inference and testing triples added as known true values, so they cannot skew the evaluation results of the model

When predicting triples you can also predict unseen triples containing entities that have yet to be seen by the model at any point, as long as the relations connecting them have already been seen during training.

This is why inductive training is even more powerful than traditional transductive traning.

data.op_id: Graph embedding and link prediction > Inductive setting > Split inductive dataset
data.title: Split inductive dataset

params.text:
### Model training
Very similar to the transductive setting, with the key difference being that here we train on the `transductive training` graph and infer on the `inductive inference` graph.

Model performance evaluation for early stopping, or in general happens on the validation set, that is why we have to provide that as well.

The rest of the settings are your normal training loop settings found in most tasks with the `training approach` being the link-prediction specific setting that is explained in the `Link prediction with PyKEEN` demo.

This model definition box has a few special parameters related to the `NodePiece` representation. (c.f. [NodePiece paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.12144)).

In short conventional embedding representations will assign a unique vector to each entity. This results in linear memory consumption. However with `NodePiece` a vocabulary of subword/sub-entity units is constructed from anchor nodes in a
graph with known relation types. Given such a fixed-size vocabulary, it is possible
to bootstrap an encoding and embedding for any entity, including those unseen
during training.

When `use GNN` is toggled the same Inductive NodePiece model is used, enhanced with a GNN encoder on top.
Overall, it’s a 3-step procedure:
 - Featurizing nodes via `NodePiece`
 - Message passing over the active graph using `NodePiece` features
 - Scoring function for a given batch of triples

As of now, message passing is expected to be over the full graph.
Defaults to 2-layer `CompGCN` with Hadamard composition.
